summary: Atwood suggests that reviewing code you wrote five years ago provides a humbling perspective on your growth as a developer. He reflects on his own VBScript and Classic ASP code from 2001, noting how difficult it is to distinguish personal mistakes from limitations imposed by inadequate tools. He observes that improvements in frameworks and development environments have outpaced his personal skill growth. The post concludes with a forward-looking question about what blind spots we might have today that will be obvious in five years.
key_points: Reviewing old code is a valuable practice for measuring professional growth
It's often hard to separate personal coding mistakes from limitations of the tools and environments available at the time
Platform and tooling improvements can dwarf individual developer skill improvements over the same period
Developers should actively seek out alternative development environments to avoid wearing blinders
The code we write today will likely look just as dated in five years
money_quotes: Go read some source code you wrote five years ago for a real scare.
My goal is to suck slightly less every year.
It's difficult to tell my personal WTFs apart from the WTFs inherited due to limitations in the WSH languages and Classic ASP coding environments.
It's like trying to choose between using an old shoe or a glass bottle to hammer nails.
I also knew I was wearing blinders – and I also knew .NET was right around the corner. But what's around the next corner?
How will I look back on today's code five years from now?
key_insight: The embarrassment we feel reading old code comes partly from our growth as developers, but equally from the primitive tools we were forced to use—and we should assume we're wearing similar blinders today.
